coredata.dc:title: A highly sensitive fluorescent detection method utilizing B and S co-doped graphene quantum dots for ibuprofen analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.
coredata.dc:creator: Geng, Zheyi
coredata.dc:description: A highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe utilizing B and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (BS-GQDs) has been created for detecting ibuprofen. The co-doping strategy effectively modulated the electronic structure and surface properties of GQDs, resulting in enhanced fluorescence quantum yield and sensing performance. The BS-GQDs exhibited strong fluorescence quenching upon interaction with ibuprofen, which was attributed to the formation of a ground-state complex and electron transfer from BS-GQDs to ibuprofen. Under optimal conditions, the sensor exhibited a broad linear range, a low detection limit, and outstanding selectivity for ibuprofen compared to other common medications and interferents. The practical utility of the sensor was validated through the successful identification of ibuprofen in spiked urine and serum samples, achieving high recovery rates and accuracy. The BS-GQDs-based fluorescent sensor provides a promising platform for sensitive, rapid, and reliable monitoring of ibuprofen in pharmaceutical and biomedical fields.
coredata.openaccessSponsorName: CRUI: Gold journals 2023-2024 discount after quota
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heteroatom doping
Fluorescence quenching
Electron transfer
Biological samples
Pharmaceutical analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical properties of BS-GQDs
Highly sensitive ibuprofen detection using BS-GQDs

xocs:oa-sponsor.xocs:oa-sponsor-name: CRUI: Gold journals 2023-2024 discount after quota

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 The Author. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Faculty of Engineering, Alexandria University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, and (c) size distribution of BS-GQDs.
XPS spectra of BS-GQDs: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) B 1 s, and (d) S 2p.
(a) UV–vis absorption and (b) fluorescence emission spectra of BS-GQDs.

(a) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of BS-GQDs. (b) Effect of pH on the quenching efficiency of ibuprofen (100 μM) on BS-GQDs. (c) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of BS-GQDs by ibuprofen (100 μM).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of BS-GQDs upon addition of ibuprofen (0–500 μM). (b) Stern-Volmer plot of F0/F versus ibuprofen concentration.
Calibration curve for ibuprofen detection using BS-GQDs.
UV–vis absorption spectra of BS-GQDs in the absence and presence of ibuprofen (100 μM).

(a) Selectivity of the BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen over other common drugs (100 μM each). (b) Quenching efficiency of ibuprofen (100 μM) on BS-GQDs in the presence of other drugs (100 μM each) in binary and ternary mixtures.
Effect of potential interfering substances (100 μM each) on the fluorescence intensity and the quenching efficiency of ibuprofen (100 μM) on BS-GQDs.
Analytical performance of the BS-GQDs sensor compared to other sensors in detecting ibuprofen.
Fluorescence decay characteristics of BS-GQDs with and without varying concentrations of ibuprofen.
Recovery of ibuprofen in human urine and serum samples using the BS-GQDs sensor.

head.ce:title: A highly sensitive fluorescent detection method utilizing B and S co-doped graphene quantum dots for ibuprofen analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Biological Sciences and Veterinary Medicine, Carmelino University, Camerino, Macerata 62032, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Sciences and Veterinary Medicine, Carmelino University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Biological Sciences and Veterinary Medicine, Carmelino University, Camerino, Macerata 62032 Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly sensitive and selective fluorescent probe utilizing B and S co-doped graphene quantum dots (BS-GQDs) has been created for detecting ibuprofen. The co-doping strategy effectively modulated the electronic structure and surface properties of GQDs, resulting in enhanced fluorescence quantum yield and sensing performance. The BS-GQDs exhibited strong fluorescence quenching upon interaction with ibuprofen, which was attributed to the formation of a ground-state complex and electron transfer from BS-GQDs to ibuprofen. Under optimal conditions, the sensor exhibited a broad linear range, a low detection limit, and outstanding selectivity for ibuprofen compared to other common medications and interferents. The practical utility of the sensor was validated through the successful identification of ibuprofen in spiked urine and serum samples, achieving high recovery rates and accuracy. The BS-GQDs-based fluorescent sensor provides a promising platform for sensitive, rapid, and reliable monitoring of ibuprofen in pharmaceutical and biomedical fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Ibuprofen, a frequently utilized non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID), is often prescribed to alleviate pain, reduce inflammation, and lower fever. It is also available over-the-counter for the relief of mild to moderate pain and menstrual cramps [1]. Due to its widespread use, monitoring ibuprofen levels in biological fluids is of great importance for patient care, drug dosage adjustment, and prevention of adverse effects associated with overdose or chronic use [2,3]. Consequently, the exploration of sensitive and selective techniques for detecting and measuring ibuprofen in intricate biological samples has been a focal point of scientific investigation. Conventional analytical techniques for ibuprofen detection include chromatographic methods such as HPLC [4,5], GC [6,7], and LC-MS [8,9]. While these methods offer excellent precision and dependability, they often face challenges such as complex sample preparation, lengthy analysis times, increased expenses, and the need for experienced operators and sophisticated equipment. Electrochemical methods have also been explored for ibuprofen sensing, but they may lack selectivity due to interferences from other electroactive species present in biological samples [10,11].

In recent years, fluorescence-based detection techniques have gained increasing attention as promising alternatives for drug analysis owing to their high sensitivity, simplicity, rapid response, and potential for miniaturization and on-site detection [12–15]. Fluorescent nanomaterials, particularly quantum dots (QDs), have gained attention as promising probes for sensing applications because of their distinctive optical characteristics. These include wide absorption spectra, narrow and adjustable emission spectra, high quantum yields, and superior photostability when compared to conventional organic dyes [16–18]. The fluorescence of QDs can be quenched or enhanced upon interaction with target analytes, providing a sensitive and selective means for detection. Among various QD systems, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have garnered significant interest as fluorescent probes for biosensing and environmental monitoring [19–21]. GQDs are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, composed of a single or few layers of graphene sheets. They exhibit excellent water solubility, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and resistance to photobleaching, making them suitable for applications in biological systems [22]. Additionally, GQDs' optical characteristics can be adjusted through changes in their dimensions, morphology, and surface properties, as well as by incorporating different elements into the graphene structure [23]. Recent advances in the synthesis and functionalization of GQDs have opened up new possibilities for enhancing their fluorescence sensing performance [24]. In particular, doping GQDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, and boron has been shown to modulate their electronic structure, increase quantum yield, and provide additional binding sites for target analytes. For example, nitrogen-doped GQDs have been using for the sensing of d Fe3+ ions [25]. Sulfur-doped GQDs have demonstrated improved selectivity towards quercetin compared to undoped counterparts [26]. Boron doping has been reported to enhance the fluorescence intensity and quantum yield of GQDs, enabling their use as sensors for oxytetracycline and other biomolecules [27]. However, the potential of co-doping GQDs with multiple heteroatoms for fluorescence sensing of drugs such as ibuprofen has not been fully explored.

This study describes the synthesis of graphene quantum dots co-doped with boron and sulfur (BS-GQDs) and their application as fluorescent sensors for detecting ibuprofen. The BS-GQDs were produced through a simple one-step pyrolysis process using citric acid as the carbon source, and boric acid and mercaptopropionic acid as the boron and sulfur sources, respectively. While previous studies have explored single-element doping of GQDs, our work is the first to investigate the synergistic effects of boron and sulfur co-doping for enhancing ibuprofen sensing. Boron doping increases the electron-donating ability of GQDs, while sulfur doping introduces additional energy levels and surface defects. The combination of these effects results in a higher fluorescence quantum yield and improved sensitivity compared to single-element doped or undoped GQDs. Furthermore, the boron-sulfur co-doping strategy creates unique surface functionalities that enhance the specific interaction with ibuprofen molecules. This leads to improved selectivity and a lower detection limit compared to previously reported GQD-based sensors. Our approach also offers advantages in terms of simplified synthesis and enhanced stability of the sensing platform.

BS-GQDs were produced through a single-step pyrolysis process from the bottom up [28]. The synthesis was carried out using a programmable muffle furnace calibrated using a type K thermocouple. Citric acid (2.1 g), boric acid (0.3 g), and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (0.4 mL) were precisely weighed using an analytical balance and mixed in a 50 mL borosilicate glass beaker. The mixture was heated at 200°C for 30 minutes, with temperature monitored continuously. Upon completion, the dark brown viscous liquid was cooled to room temperature and dissolved in 50 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution prepared using deionized water. The pH was adjusted to 7.0 using a calibrated pH meterThe solution was then dialyzed against ultrapure water using a 1 kDa MWCO dialysis membrane for 24 hours, with water changed every 6 hours. The final product was collected and stored in amber glass vials at 4°C until further use.

Stock solutions of ibuprofen (100 mM) and other drugs were prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of analyte in ethanol and storing at 4 °C. The stock solutions were diluted with PBS to prepare the working solutions before being used. During a typical assay, 100 μL of BS-GQDs solution (0.1 mg/mL) was combined with 100 μL of ibuprofen solution.

The selectivity of the proposed sensor was tested by comparing the fluorescence response of BS-GQDs towards ibuprofen and other common drugs, including acetaminophen, aspirin, diclofenac, ketoprofen, and naproxen, at the same concentration level (100 μM). The fluorescence intensity in the presence of ibuprofen (100 μM) and various interferents (100 μM) was measured under the same assay conditions.

The BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen detection in biological fluids was assessed using urine and serum samples. The urine and serum samples were artificially contaminated with varying levels of ibuprofen (10, 50, and 100 μM) and then diluted with PBS at a ratio of 1:10 prior to testing. The recovery experiments were performed by adding 100 μL of the diluted spiked samples to 100 μL of BS-GQDs solution. The recovery percentage was calculated by comparing the measured ibuprofen concentration with the spiked concentration, and the results were validated by HPLC analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of BS-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the as-synthesized BS-GQDs were investigated by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1a, the BS-GQDs exhibit a uniform spherical shape with a narrow size distribution. The HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) reveals the lattice fringes of the BS-GQDs, indicating their crystalline nature. The statistical analysis of more than 100 particles indicates that the average diameter of the BS-GQDs is approximately 3.5 nm with a margin of error of 0.6 nm (Fig. 1c). The distribution is slightly positively skewed (skewness coefficient = 0.32), indicating a tail towards larger particle sizes. The small size and uniform distribution of the BS-GQDs are attributed to the effective control of the nucleation and growth processes during the bottom-up synthesis [29].

The XPS survey spectrum ( Fig. 2a) of BS-GQDs shows characteristic peaks at 284.8, 532.2, 191.5, and 163.8 eV, corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, B 1 s, and S 2p, respectively. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b) have four peaks at 284.5, 285.6, 286.3, and 288.8 eV, which are assigned to CC, C-B, C-S, and CO/C-O-C bonds [30], respectively. The B 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c) exhibits a main peak at 191.2 eV, suggesting the presence of B-C bonds [31]. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) can be fitted with two peaks at 163.6 and 164.8 eV, which are attributed to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, suggesting the existence of C-S-C bonds [32].

The FTIR spectrum of BS-GQDs ( Fig. 3) displays characteristic absorption bands at 3420, 1730, 1630, 1400, 1220, and 1080 cm−1. The peak at 3420 cm−1 is attributed to the O-H and N-H groups. The peaks at 1730 and 1630 cm−1 correspond to the CO and CC [33], respectively. The 1400 cm-1 band is attributed to the B-O bond, whereas the 1220 cm-1 peak is linked to the C-O-C bond [34].The C-S bond is linked to the absorption peak at 1080 cm−1 [35]. These XPS and FTIR results collectively demonstrate the successful co-doping of B and S into the GQDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum of BS-GQDs shows a main peak at 288 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains ( Fig. 4a). Moreover, a peak at approximately 330 nm is associated with the n-π* transition of carbonyl groups [36]. The fluorescence emission spectrum of BS-GQDs (Fig. 4b) reveals a strong peak at 440 nm when excited at 350 nm, with a FWHM of 80 nm. The narrow emission bandwidth and the large Stokes shift (90 nm) of BS-GQDs are indicative of their uniform surface states and reduced self-quenching effect [37].

The quantification of BS-GQDs was assessed by comparing it to quinine sulfate as a standard reference. The QY of BS-GQDs is calculated to be 19.8 %, which is significantly higher than that of undoped GQDs (7.5 %) prepared under the same conditions. The enhanced QY of BS-GQDs is due to the synergistic effect of boron and sulfur co-doping, which modulates the electronic structure and surface properties of GQDs. Boron doping is known to increase the electron-donating ability and reduce the work function of GQDs, while sulfur doping can introduce additional energy levels and surface defects that serve as radiative recombination centers. The co-doping strategy effectively passivates the non-radiative recombination sites and improves the fluorescence emission efficiency of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Highly sensitive ibuprofen detection using BS-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve the best sensing performance, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of BS-GQDs and the quenching efficiency of ibuprofen was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity remains fairly constant within the pH range of 6.0–8.0. However, a notable decline in intensity is observed at pH levels below 6.0 or above 8.0. This occurrence is a result of the functional groups on the surface of BS-GQDs being protonated or deprotonated, leading to changes in their surface charge and fluorescence characteristics [38]. The quenching efficiency of ibuprofen (100 μM) on BS-GQDs is found to be highest at pH 7 (Fig. 5b), which is close to the physiological pH and favors the interaction between the drug and the fluorescent probe. Therefore, pH 7 is selected. The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence quenching of BS-GQDs by ibuprofen (100 μM) was also studied. As depicted in Fig. 5c, the quenching efficiency reaches a plateau after 10 min of incubation, indicating a rapid response of the sensor to the analyte. Consequently, an incubation time of 10 min is chosen for the assay to ensure a stable signal and a fast detection process.

The Stern-Volmer equation [39] was used to quantitatively assess the fluorescence quenching of BS-GQDs caused by ibuprofen [39]. As shown in Fig. 6a, the fluorescence intensity of BS-GQDs decreases with increasing concentrations of ibuprofen from 0 to 500 μM. The Stern-Volmer plot (Fig. 6b) exhibits a good linear relationship between F0/F and [Q], with a R2 of 0.9989. The Ksv value is determined to be 1.2 × 104 M−1, indicating a high sensitivity of the BS-GQDs sensor towards ibuprofen.

The calibration curve for ibuprofen detection using BS-GQDs is constructed by plotting the relative fluorescence intensity (F/F0) against the logarithm of ibuprofen concentration ( Fig. 7). The linear regression equation is F/F0 = −0.1807 log[Q] + 0.4713, with a R2 of 0.9972. The linear detection range spans from 0.1 to 500 μM, covering more than three orders of magnitude. The LOD is calculated to be 30 nM. The performance of the BS-GQDs sensor are superior to those of many previously reported fluorescent probes for ibuprofen ( Table 1). The BS-GQDs sensor exhibits several advantages over these probes. Moreover, the BS-GQDs sensor is based on a simple and green synthetic approach, without the need for complex modification or functionalization steps. The excellent sensing performance and facile preparation of BS-GQDs make them a promising candidate for practical applications in pharmaceutical analysis and drug monitoring.

UV–vis absorption spectroscopy was employed to investigate the interaction between the fluorescent probe and the analyte. The BS-GQDs absorption spectrum shows a distinct peak at 288 nm, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of the conjugated aromatic regions ( Fig. 8a). Upon addition of ibuprofen (100 μM), a slight red-shift (2 nm) and a decrease in the absorbance intensity are observed, indicating the formation of a ground-state complex between BS-GQDs and ibuprofen [47]. The formation of the complex is further confirmed by the appearance of a new band at around 250 nm, which is attributed to the charge transfer from the electron-rich BS-GQDs to the electron-deficient ibuprofen molecule. The ground-state complex formation is likely driven by the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding between the carboxylate group of ibuprofen and the hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of BS-GQDs [48].

Time-resolved fluorescence measurements were conducted to analyze the fluorescence decay behavior. As summarized in Table 2, the average lifetime of BS-GQDs decreases from 5.68 ns to 3.32 ns upon addition of 200 μM ibuprofen, confirming the dynamic quenching mechanism. The dynamic quenching occurs through the collision between the excited-state BS-GQDs and the ground-state ibuprofen molecules, which facilitates the non-radiative decay of the fluorophore. The quenching rate constant (kq) value suggests a highly efficient quenching process, which is consistent with the strong fluorescence quenching observed in the steady-state measurements.

This work performed fluorescence anisotropy measurements to investigate the formation of a ground-state complex between BS-GQDs and ibuprofen. The anisotropy values increased from 0.12 to 0.28 upon addition of ibuprofen, indicating restricted rotational motion of BS-GQDs due to complex formation. Additionally, this work carried out circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy, which revealed the emergence of a new band at 275 nm when ibuprofen was added to BS-GQDs, further confirming the formation of a chiral complex. To elucidate the electron transfer mechanism, this work conducted transient absorption spectroscopy. The results showed a fast decay component (τ = 2.3 ps) in the presence of ibuprofen, which can be attributed to electron transfer from BS-GQDs to ibuprofen. This observation was further supported by electrochemical measurements using cyclic voltammetry, which demonstrated a shift in the reduction potential of BS-GQDs upon addition of ibuprofen.

The selectivity of the BS-GQDs sensor towards ibuprofen was evaluated by comparing its fluorescence response to other common drugs, including acetaminophen, aspirin, diclofenac, ketoprofen, and naproxen. As shown in Fig. 9a, the addition of ibuprofen (100 μM) leads to a significant quenching of the fluorescence intensity, with a quenching efficiency of 78 %. In contrast, the other drugs at the same concentration exhibit much lower quenching efficiencies, ranging from 3 % to 15 %. The high selectivity of the BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen is attributed to the specific interaction between the carboxylate group of ibuprofen and the surface functional groups of BS-GQDs, as well as the favorable electron transfer from BS-GQDs to ibuprofen. The structural differences between ibuprofen and the other drugs, such as the absence of a carboxylate group or the presence of additional functional groups, may hinder their binding to BS-GQDs and reduce the quenching efficiency.

To further demonstrate the selectivity of the sensor, competitive experiments were carried out by measuring the fluorescence response of BS-GQDs to ibuprofen (100 μM) in the presence of other drugs (100 μM each). As depicted in Fig. 9b, the quenching efficiency of ibuprofen remains almost unchanged (75–80 %) in the binary and ternary mixtures, indicating that the presence of other drugs does not interfere with the detection of ibuprofen. The excellent selectivity of the BS-GQDs sensor makes it a promising tool for the specific determination of ibuprofen.

The anti-interference ability of the BS-GQDs sensor was tested by assessing the influence of potential interfering substances on the fluorescence quenching of BS-GQDs by ibuprofen. The tested interferents include common ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Zn2+), biomolecules (glucose, lactose, sucrose, citric acid, ascorbic acid, uric acid), and matrix components (human serum albumin, globulin). As shown in Fig. 10, the presence of these interferents at a concentration of 100 μM has negligible effects on the fluorescence intensity, with variations of less than 5 %. Moreover, the quenching efficiency of ibuprofen (100 μM) on BS-GQDs remains stable (75–80 %) in the presence of the interferents, suggesting that the sensor can selectively detect ibuprofen without significant interference from the coexisting substances.

The high anti-interference ability of the BS-GQDs sensor is ascribed to the following reasons: (1) the specific recognition of ibuprofen by BS-GQDs through hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions; (2) the selective electron transfer quenching mechanism that is less affected by the non-quenching interferents; and (3) the surface passivation effect of the heteroatom doping that reduces the non-specific adsorption of interferents on BS-GQDs. These features collectively contribute to the reliable and accurate detection of ibuprofen in complex matrices using the BS-GQDs sensor.

To evaluate the reproducibility and stability of the BS-GQDs sensor, a series of experiments were conducted. The sensor's performance was assessed across multiple trials, different days, and various operators. Ten independent batches of BS-GQDs were synthesized and used for ibuprofen sensing assays at 100 μM. The fluorescence quenching efficiency showed excellent reproducibility with a RSD of 3.8 %. Inter-day variability was examined by repeating calibration curve measurements on five consecutive days, resulting in an RSD of 4.2 % for the slopes of the calibration curves.

The BS-GQDs were stored at 4°C, room temperature (25°C), and 37°C, both in dark and light conditions. The fluorescence intensity and ibuprofen sensing performance were measured at regular intervals. The results show that the BS-GQDs maintain over 95 % of their initial fluorescence intensity and sensing capability when stored at 4°C in the dark for 30 days. At room temperature, the stability slightly decreased, retaining 90 % functionality after 30 days. Storage at 37°C or exposure to light led to more pronounced decreases in performance.

The urine and serum samples were diluted 10-fold to minimize the matrix effects and then spiked with ibuprofen at three concentration levels (10, 50, and 100 μM). The fluorescence intensity of BS-GQDs was measured before and after the addition of the spiked samples, and the recovery was calculated.

As summarized in Table 3, the recoveries of ibuprofen in urine samples range from 95.6 % to 104.2 %, with RSDs of less than 3.5 %. For serum samples, the recoveries are between 96.3 % and 105.1 %, with RSDs below 4.2 %. These results demonstrate the good accuracy and precision of the BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen detection in biological fluids. The high recoveries and low RSDs indicate that the matrix effects are effectively minimized by sample dilution and the selective response of the sensor to ibuprofen.

The reliability of the BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen detection in biological samples was further validated by comparing its results with those obtained from a standard analytical method, namely HPLC. Three urine and serum samples spiked with ibuprofen were analyzed by both the BS-GQDs sensor and HPLC. The ibuprofen concentrations determined by the BS-GQDs sensor are in good agreement with those measured by HPLC, with relative errors of less than 5 %. The R2 between the two methods is 0.9989, confirming the accuracy of the BS-GQDs sensor for ibuprofen quantification in complex biological matrices.

Compared with HPLC, the BS-GQDs sensor offers several advantages, such as simplicity, rapidity, cost-effectiveness, and high sensitivity. The fluorescence detection can be performed using a conventional spectrofluorometer or microplate reader. Moreover, the BS-GQDs sensor shows a wider linear range and lower LOD than HPLC, enabling the sensitive detection of ibuprofen at trace levels. These features make the BS-GQDs sensor a promising alternative to traditional analytical methods for ibuprofen monitoring in clinical and pharmaceutical applications.

This work developed a novel fluorescent sensor based on BS-GQDs for the sensing of ibuprofen. The BS-GQDs were synthesized by a facile bottom-up pyrolysis approach and exhibited excellent optical properties, including high QY, narrow emission bandwidth, and large Stokes shift. The BS-GQDs showed a strong fluorescence quenching response towards ibuprofen. The quenching mechanism was explored through UV–vis absorption and fluorescence lifetime measurements. The results indicated the formation of a ground-state complex between BS-GQDs and ibuprofen, as well as a dynamic quenching process involving electron transfer. The BS-GQDs sensor exhibited excellent selectivity for ibuprofen over other common drugs and interferents, as well as high recovery and accuracy in spiked urine and serum samples, as validated by HPLC. The BS-GQDs sensor offers a simple, rapid, sensitive, and reliable approach for ibuprofen detection in pharmaceutical and biomedical applications, and represents a promising platform for the development of advanced fluorescent probes for drug monitoring and analysis.

Zheyi Geng: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simple and rapid detection of ibuprofen─a typical pharmaceuticals and personal care products─by a liquid crystal aptasensor
Langmuir

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Yang, Z. Yang, Simple and Rapid Detection of Ibuprofen─A Typical Pharmaceuticals and Personal Care Products─by a Liquid Crystal Aptasensor, Langmuir 38 (2022) 282–288. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.langmuir.1c02480.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection and degradation of ibuprofen from water on multi-walled carbon nanotubes-epoxy composite electrode
J. Environ. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Motoc, A. Remes, A. Pop, F. Manea, J. Schoonman, Electrochemical detection and degradation of ibuprofen from water on multi-walled carbon nanotubes-epoxy composite electrode, Journal of Environmental Sciences 25 (2013) 838–847. https://doi.org/10.1016/S1001-0742(12)60068-0.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver-functionalized carbon nanofiber composite electrodes for ibuprofen detection
Nanoscale Res. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Manea, S. Motoc, A. Pop, A. Remes, J. Schoonman, Silver-functionalized carbon nanofiber composite electrodes for ibuprofen detection, Nanoscale Res Lett 7 (2012) 331. https://doi.org/10.1186/1556-276X-7-331.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: HPLC determination of ibuprofen, diclofenac and salicylic acid using hollow fiber-based liquid phase microextraction (HF-LPME)
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.R. Payán, M.Á.B. López, R. Fernández-Torres, J.L.P. Bernal, M.C. Mochón, HPLC determination of ibuprofen, diclofenac and salicylic acid using hollow fiber-based liquid phase microextraction (HF-LPME), Analytica Chimica Acta 653 (2009) 184–190. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2009.09.018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development and validation of an HPLC method for simultaneous determination of ibuprofen and 17 related compounds
Chromatographia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Han, L. Lu, L. Wang, Z. Yan, X. Wang, Development and Validation of an HPLC Method for Simultaneous Determination of Ibuprofen and 17 Related Compounds, Chromatographia 80 (2017) 1353–1360. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10337-017-3358-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gas chromatographic and spectrophotometric determination of diclofenac sodium, ibuprofen, and mefenamic acid in urine and blood samples
Turk. J. Pharm. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N.S. JALBANI, A.R. SOLANGI, M.Y. KHUHAWAR, S. MEMON, R. JUNEJO, A.A. MEMON, Gas Chromatographic and Spectrophotometric Determination of Diclofenac Sodium, Ibuprofen, and Mefenamic Acid in Urine and Blood Samples, Turk J Pharm Sci 17 (2020) 465–473. https://doi.org/10.4274/tjps.galenos.2019.42243.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of ibuprofen in human plasma and urine by gas chromatography/mass spectrometry
J. AOAC Int.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Yilmaz, A.F. Erdem, Determination of Ibuprofen in Human Plasma and Urine by Gas Chromatography/Mass Spectrometry, Journal of AOAC INTERNATIONAL 97 (2014) 415–420. https://doi.org/10.5740/jaoacint.11-414.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: LC–MS–MS determination of ibuprofen, 2-hydroxyibuprofen enantiomers, and carboxyibuprofen stereoisomers for application in biotransformation studies employing endophytic fungi
